pathophysiology_summary: Tinea corporis is a dermatophyte infection of glabrous skin (here zoophilic, from a newly adopted kitten) producing annular, pruritic, erythematous plaques with a raised scaly advancing (active) border, central clearing and sometimes pustules at the margin, expanding centrifugally. Topical corticosteroid (OTC hydrocortisone) suppresses local immunity and accelerates and atypically modifies the spread — 'tinea incognito' — so it must be stopped. Discriminators: a prominent scaly, pruritic advancing border with central clearing distinguishes it from the smooth, non-scaly, usually asymptomatic expanding rash of erythema migrans (Lyme); central clearing with an active raised edge distinguishes it from uniformly inflamed nummular eczema; and marginal pustules with animal contact and asymmetry argue against pityriasis rosea. Management is stopping the steroid, a topical antifungal (terbinafine) applied to and roughly 2 cm beyond the active border, continued about 1-2 weeks past clearance, itch relief, hygiene and no towel sharing, and checking the kitten; a short oral antifungal is reserved for extensive, refractory or markedly steroid-modified disease. Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection.

differential.evidence_basis: Annular scaly advancing-border plaques with central clearing after kitten contact.
An expanding annular rash — but erythema migrans is smooth, non-scaly and usually not itchy.
Coin-shaped uniformly inflamed plaques, typically without the clear centre seen here.
Herald patch with a Christmas-tree distribution, not asymmetrical annular scaly rings with marginal pustules.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognise tinea corporis (annular scaly advancing border with central clearing), stop the topical steroid, and treat with an antifungal extended past the border; keep erythema migrans / Lyme briefly on the differential.

differential.evidence_basis: Scaly advancing border with central clearing, steroid-modified; secondary-infection screen negative.
A scaly, pruritic border with central clearing is not erythema migrans, which is smooth and non-scaly.
Working diagnosis: stop the steroid, topical antifungal to and past the border (± short oral course if extensive), itch relief, hygiene and vet check; secondary-infection safety-net.

red_flags.description: Secondary bacterial infection (hot or hard swelling, thick yellow pus, or fever)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would need antibiotics and prompt in-person review.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not stopping the topical corticosteroid that drives tinea incognito, allowing continued atypical spread.
Not applying the antifungal beyond the visible border, allowing the active edge to keep advancing.
Not advising a vet check for the kitten, leading to reinfection or household spread.
Escalating benign dermatophytosis to urgent in-person or ED assessment instead of antifungal self-care plus routine review with a safety-net.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Stop the steroid, prescribe a topical antifungal to and beyond the active border, itch relief, hygiene and a vet check; short oral antifungal if extensive or refractory; prompt review / urgent care only if secondary infection develops.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Continuing steroids (worsening tinea incognito) or missing a secondary bacterial infection.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tinea corporis: topical antifungal (e.g. terbinafine) applied to and beyond the active border; oral antifungal for extensive, refractory or steroid-modified disease; stop topical corticosteroids; treat sources and contacts.
